4: shema yisrael YHWH eloheinu YHWH echad (šĕmaʿ yiśrāʾēl Yhwh ʾĕlōhênû Yhwh ʾeḥād): 'Hear O Israel: YHWH our God, YHWH is one.' The Shema is the foundational confession of Jewish faith, recited morning and evening by observant Jews. Echad (one) is the standard Hebrew numeral one — it allows for internal distinction (as in yom echad, one day, composed of evening and morning; Genesis 2:24, basar echad, one flesh, composed of two persons) but asserts the unity of the divine being against all polytheism. Paul's expansion in 1 Corinthians 8:6 ('one God the Father ... and one Lord Jesus Christ') is not an abandonment of monotheism but a Christological reconfiguration: the Shema's single divine identity now encompasses both Father and Son.
1: הַמִּצְוָה הַחֻקִּים וְהַמִּשְׁפָּטִים (hammitsĕwāh haḥuqqîm wĕhammishpāṭîm) — the three categories of Deuteronomic law: the overarching commandment (mitswāh, covenant loyalty), the statutes (ḥuqqîm, ordinances without stated reason), and the rules (mishpāṭîm, case-law judgments). The singular mitswāh can function as a collective for the whole Torah or as the one great demand (love of YHWH, v5) that the rest expresses.
2: לְיִרְאָה (lĕyirʾāh) — "to fear." Yirʾāh (H3374) in Deuteronomy encompasses both reverential awe and practical obedience — it is not mere terror but the posture of standing before a sovereign whose word is authoritative. The goal stated here — fear → keeping → long life — traces the covenant logic: the proper orientation toward YHWH produces the behavior that results in the flourishing promised in the covenant.
3: The summative appeal of v3 captures the Deuteronomic pattern: shema (hear/obey) → shāmar (keep/guard) → ʿāśāh (do). The three verbs together form a comprehensive call: receive, internalize, and enact. "That it may go well with you" (ʾăšer yîṭab lāk) ties obedience to covenant blessing — not as merit but as the natural ecology of the covenant relationship.
5: וְאָהַבְתָּ אֵת יְהוָה אֱלֹהֶיךָ (wĕʾāhavtā ʾēt Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā) — "you shall love the LORD your God." Āhab (H157) in a covenant context denotes not merely warm feeling but the committed loyalty of a covenant partner. The love required here is structured by three terms: lĕbābĕkā (your heart — the decision-making center and will), napšĕkā (your soul/life — the whole self, including emotions and vitality), and mĕʾōdekā (your might/abundance — all your resources and energy). Jesus cites this as the greatest commandment (Matthew 22:37), adding "mind" (dianoia) which renders the Deuteronomic lēb in its full cognitive dimension. Together the three/four faculties form a merism: the entirety of human personhood is to be oriented toward YHWH.
6: עַל לְבָבֶךָ (ʿal lĕbābĕkā) — "on your heart." The location of the commandment is the heart, not a stone tablet or scroll: internalizing precedes externalizing. This verse is the precursor to the new covenant promise of Jeremiah 31:33 ("I will put my law on their hearts") — the Deuteronomic demand for heart-law becomes the covenantal goal that the Sinaitic system could specify but not guarantee.
7: וְשִׁנַּנְתָּם (wĕšinnantām) — "you shall teach them diligently/sharpen them." The verb (H8150) has the nuance of incision — sharp, repeated instruction. The contexts of teaching listed — sitting, walking, lying down, rising — are the complete round of daily life. The Torah is not to be reserved for formal instruction but woven into every conversational moment.
8: קְשַׁרְתָּם לְאוֹת (qĕšartām lĕʾôt) — "bind them as a sign on your hand." The command became the basis for the practice of tefillin (phylacteries) — small boxes containing Torah texts worn on the hand and forehead. Whether the original text intends the physical practice or a metaphor for constant Torah-mindedness (as Proverbs 3:3 uses similar language metaphorically) is debated. Jesus criticizes the Pharisaic expansion of the practice (Matthew 23:5, "they make their phylacteries broad") without dismissing the underlying principle.
9: מְזוּזוֹת (mĕzûzôt) — "doorposts." The mezuzah practice (affixing Torah texts to doorposts) arises from this command. The doorpost was already a covenantal marker from the Passover blood ritual (Exodus 12:7, 22-23). Writing Torah on the gates frames the household as a covenant space: entering and leaving, the family passes under the sign of the covenant.
10: The transition from wilderness to settled land poses the chief Deuteronomic spiritual danger: prosperity-induced forgetting. "Houses filled with all good things that you did not fill" — the unearned abundance of the land is YHWH's gift, not Israel's achievement. The ethical danger of inherited blessing is the delusion of self-sufficiency.
11: The accumulation of unearned goods — "cisterns cut out that you did not cut, vineyards and olive trees that you did not plant" — forms the material backdrop for the warning of v12. This is the classic Deuteronomic prosperity trap: the blessings of covenant faithfulness can, paradoxically, generate the forgetfulness that breaks the covenant.
12: פֶּן תִּשְׁכַּח (pen tishkaḥ) — "take care that you do not forget." The verb shākaḥ (H7911, to forget) is the negative counterpart to the constant Deuteronomic imperative to remember (zākar). Forgetting YHWH means losing the Exodus-identity that defines Israel as a people. Memory is not merely psychological but covenantal: to forget is to functionally deny the relationship.
13: אֶת יְהוָה אֱלֹהֶיךָ תִּירָא וְאֹתוֹ תַעֲבֹד (ʾet Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā tîrāʾ wĕʾōtô taʿăbōd) — "the LORD your God you shall fear, and him you shall serve." The emphatic word order places YHWH (ʾōtô) before the verbs — exclusive devotion: YHWH alone is to be feared and served. Jesus quotes this verse directly against Satan in the wilderness (Matthew 4:10: "you shall worship the Lord your God, and him only shall you serve"), using proskynēseis (worship/bow down) for tîrāʾ and latreuō (serve/render religious service) for taʿăbōd.
14: The prohibition "do not go after other gods" (lōʾ tēlĕkûn ʾaḥărê ʾĕlōhîm ʾăḥērîm) uses the verb hālak ʾaḥărê (to walk after, to follow) — the language of personal allegiance and following a lord. Going after other gods is the covenantal equivalent of desertion.
15: אֵל קַנָּא — jealous God (as in Deuteronomy 5:9). The phrase "lest the anger of the LORD your God be kindled against you and he destroy you from the face of the earth" (wĕhišmidĕkā mēʿal pĕnê hāʾădāmāh) is the ultimate covenant sanction — exclusion from covenant life and from the land that is covenant existence. The stakes of covenant unfaithfulness are not merely behavioral correction but existential annihilation of the community.
16: לֹא תְנַסּוּ (lōʾ tĕnassû) — "you shall not put to the test." Nāsāh (H5254) means to test or prove — to act as if YHWH's presence or power needs demonstration. "As you tested him at Massah" (Exodus 17:1-7, where the people demanded water as proof of YHWH's presence) describes the paradigm: covenant testing demands proof before trusting. Jesus quotes this exact verse (Matthew 4:7; Luke 4:12) against the devil's proposal to test YHWH by a staged fall requiring angelic rescue.
17: שָׁמוֹר תִּשְׁמְרוּן (shāmôr tišmĕrûn) — the infinitive absolute with the finite verb: "diligently keep" (emphatic/intensive). The doubled verbal construction is characteristic of Deuteronomy's rhetorical emphasis; it heightens the urgency of observance beyond routine compliance to vigilant guarding.
18: הַיָּשָׁר וְהַטּוֹב (hayyāshār wĕhaṭṭôb) — "the right and the good." This phrase goes beyond statutory compliance: "what is right and good in the sight of the LORD" is a moral category beyond mere legal conformity. Deuteronomy regularly supplements the legal corpus with this kind of character-orienting language, pointing to a disposition-level righteousness rather than merely a conduct-level compliance.
19: The goal of covenant obedience includes military and territorial consequences: thrusting out (hădōp) enemies as YHWH promised. The promise of land is covenantally conditioned — the people's fidelity is the channel through which YHWH's prior promise flows into realized inheritance.
20: The catechetical question — "what do these testimonies and statutes and rules mean?" — anticipates the next generation's need for covenant formation. The child's question is the occasion for the Exodus-recital (vv21-25): law is always explained by prior redemption. The statutory life of Israel is unintelligible apart from its Exodus origin. This pattern — law after narrative, command after grace — is the structure of Deuteronomy's ethics.
21: עֲבָדִים הָיִינוּ לְפַרְעֹה (ʿăbādîm hāyînû lĕparʿōh) — "we were slaves to Pharaoh." The first-person plural encompasses every generation: the Exodus narrative is the community's constitutive story, not just ancient history. Wayyōṣiʾēnû Yhwh ("and YHWH brought us out") — the divine act of liberation precedes and generates the legal obligations of the covenant.
22: The Exodus signs and wonders (ʾōtôt ûmôpĕtîm) are the empirical foundation of the covenant's credibility. YHWH is not known abstractly but through observed, narrated historical action. The "great and terrible signs" do not generate belief as proof but provide the narrative content for a faith that is already constituted by covenant relationship.
23: לְהָבִיא אֹתָנוּ (lĕhābîʾ ʾōtānû) — "to bring us in." The Exodus is not primarily an escape-from but a bringing-to: the goal is the land, not the wilderness. The liberation from Egypt has a telos — covenant life in the promised land — and the whole Torah is the charter for that life.
24: לְיִרְאָה אֶת יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵינוּ (lĕyirʾāh ʾet Yhwh ʾĕlōhênû) — "to fear the LORD our God." The purpose of the statutes is not arbitrary obedience but the formation of the fear-of-YHWH that is the beginning of wisdom (Proverbs 9:10). "For our good always" (lĕṭôb lānû kol-hayyāmîm) — covenant law is not an imposition but a gift; the commandments are structurally oriented toward the community's flourishing.
25: וּצְדָקָה תִּהְיֶה לָּנוּ (ûṣĕdāqāh tihyeh lānû) — "and it will be righteousness for us." Ṣĕdāqāh (H6666) here functions not as a forensic verdict but as a relational/ethical state: the community that keeps the covenant commandments stands in right relation (ṣedeq) with YHWH. This is not works-righteousness in a transactional sense; it is the description of what covenant faithfulness looks like from the inside. Paul's argument in Romans (esp. Deuteronomy 10:5-6) distinguishes this righteousness of the law from the righteousness that is by faith, but both operate within the framework of covenant relation.
